Box 751, Portland, OR 97207, USA ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":0,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"faj5c","text":"2Biota Holdings Limited, 10/585 Blackburn Road, Notting Hill Victoria 3168, Australia ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":0,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"9s1ep","text":"3Max Planck Institute of Biochemistry, Department of Membrane Biochemistry, Am Klopferspitz 18, 82152 Martinsried, Germany","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":0,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"2d7aa","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"d5o6m","text":"Several haloviruses have been isolated from laboratory strains of haloarchaea. Most were inadvertent discoveries, based on the spontaneous lysis of the host culture (e.g., ΦH, ΦCh1), or the detection of virus particles in purified preparations of flagella (Hs1). A more systematic approach would be to use induction by mitomycin C, and then to plaque cell supernatants on related (nonlysogenic) host strains. Indeed, this has been recently used to isolate a new halovirus, SNJ1 (Mei et al. 2007), from a strain of Natrinema, and could be used more widely.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"2eb7u","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"18fmk","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}
